concept.headline: Patterns destructure values and bind names — match, if let, and while let all use them.
concept.explanation: A pattern describes the shape of a value. When a pattern matches, Rust binds the named parts of the pattern to the corresponding parts of the value. Patterns appear in match arms, if let, while let, function parameters, and let bindings. They can match ranges, literals, enum variants, struct fields, tuples, and include guards (extra if conditions).
keyPoints: Patterns are tested top to bottom; the first matching arm runs
Guards: pattern if condition — checked only if the pattern already matches
if let Some(v) = opt { } is a single-arm match that ignores None
while let Some(v) = iter.next() { } loops until the pattern fails
.. in struct/tuple patterns ignores remaining fields

demo.intro: Watch match categorise a number using range patterns, then a guard, then a wildcard.

explanation.why: We test categorise() with four values to see all four match arms
explanation.change: Loop starts; n will be -3, 0, 7, 42 in turn
explanation.intent: Drive the visualizer through each pattern branch
explanation.misconception: [-3, 0, 7, 42] is an array literal — Rust infers its type as [i32; 4]

explanation.why: -3 falls in the range MIN to -1 inclusive
explanation.intent: Show how a range pattern covers an open-ended range
explanation.misconception: i32::MIN is -2,147,483,648 — this pattern covers ALL negative i32 values, not just those near -3

explanation.why: categorise(-3) returned 'negative'; println! formats it with n
explanation.misconception: The function returns a &'static str — a string slice pointing to a string literal baked into the binary, not heap-allocated

explanation.why: Literal patterns match exact values — 0 only matches the integer 0
explanation.misconception: The range arm MIN..=-1 was checked first and did NOT match 0 — patterns are tested in order
The order of arms matters — if 0 appeared after 1..=9, it would never be reached (though the compiler would warn about unreachable patterns)

explanation.intent: Show inclusive range pattern for single-digit positives
explanation.misconception: 1..=9 in a pattern is NOT a range object — it is a range pattern that only exists at compile time for matching
The range 1..=9 covers exactly 1 through 9 — 10 would fall through to the wildcard

explanation.why: 42 did not match any earlier pattern, so _ catches it — _ discards the value without binding it
explanation.misconception: _ is required when there are unhandled values — without it, the compiler rejects the match as non-exhaustive

explanation.why: categorise(42) fell through to the wildcard and returned 'large'
explanation.misconception: The _ arm MUST be last — placing it first would match everything and the compiler would warn about unreachable patterns below it

prediction.explanation: t is (2, -1). The first guard: x == y → 2 == -1 is false. The second guard: x + y == 0 → 2 + (-1) = 1 ≠ 0... wait, 2 + (-1) = 1. So both guards fail and 'other' prints.

explanation.afterDemo: match tested each arm top to bottom — range patterns for negatives and single digits, an exact literal for zero, and a wildcard for everything else. Patterns make the compiler guarantee that all cases are covered, so you never miss an edge case that would cause a runtime crash.
keyTakeaways: Patterns are tested in order — first match wins.
Guards (if condition) add extra checks after the structural pattern matches.
_ (wildcard) is the catch-all; it must be the last arm.

practice.prompt:
Write a match on an i32 variable `score` (set to 85) that prints:
- "A" if 90..=100
- "B" if 75..=89
- "C" if 60..=74
- "F" otherwise
practice.hint: Use inclusive range patterns: 90..=100 => println!("A"), etc.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! 85 falls in 75..=89, so the B arm fires.
retryHints: Range patterns use ..= for inclusive end: 75..=89 covers 75, 76, …, 89.
Make sure you include a wildcard _ arm at the end — required for exhaustiveness.

summary.title: Pattern Matching — Complete
takeaways: Patterns are tested top to bottom — place specific patterns before wide ones.
Guards (if condition) refine pattern matching with extra logic.
Use if let for a single-arm match; while let for looping over an iterator.
